full_notes:
The Mughal Empire is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 39 THE MUGHALS Ruling as large a territory as the Indian subcontinent with such a diversity of people and cultures was an the Middle Ages.

The text explains that quite in contrast to their predecessors, the Mughals created an empire and accomplished what had hitherto seemed possible for only short periods of time.

The text explains that from the latter half of the sixteenth century, they expanded their kingdom from Agra and Delhi, until in the seventeenth century they controlled nearly all of the subcontinent.

The text explains that they imposed structures of administration and ideas of governance that outlasted their rule, leaving a political legacy that succeeding rulers of the subcontinent could not ignore.

The text explains that today the Prime Minister of India addresses the nation on Independence Day from the ramparts of the Red Fort in Delhi, the residence of the Mughal emperors.

The text explains that 3 Cannons were an important addition in sixteenth-century warfare.

The text explains that babur used them effectively in the Who were the Mughals?

The text explains that the Mughals were descendants of two great lineages of rulers.

The text explains that from their mother’s side they were descendants of Genghis Khan (died 1227), the Mongol ruler who ruled over parts of China and Central Asia.

The text explains that from their father’s side, they were the successors of Timur (died 1404), the ruler of Iran, Iraq and modern-day Turkey.

The text explains that however, the Mughals did not like to be called Mughal or Mongol.

The text explains that this was because Genghis Khan’s memory was associated with the massacre of innumerable people.

The text explains that it was also linked with the Uzbegs, their Mongol competitors.

The text explains that on the other hand, the Mughals were proud of their Timurid ancestry, not least of all because their great ancestor had captured They celebrated their genealogy pictorially, each ruler getting a picture made of Timur and himself.

The text explains that mughal Military Campaigns he was only 12 years old.

The text explains that he was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of another Mongol group, the Uzbegs.

The text explains that they only had rights to the revenue of their assignments which was collected for them by their servants while the themselves served in some other part of the country.

The text explains that zabt and Zamindars The main source of income available to Mughal rulers was tax on the produce of the peasantry.

The text explains that akbar Nama and Ain-i Akbari Akbar ordered one of his close friends and courtiers, Abul Fazl, to write a history of his reign.

The text explains that abul Fazl wrote a three-volume history of Akbar’s reign, titled Akbar Nama.

The text explains that the poorest amongst them lived from hand to mouth and they could hardly consider investing in additional resources – tools and supplies –

The text explains that the enormous wealth and resources commanded by the Mughal elite made them an extremely powerful group of people in the late seventeenth century.

The text explains that 47 THE MUGHALS (b) If determined a rank and salary, indicated his (c) Abul Fazl, Akbar’s friend and counsellor, helped him frame the idea of so that he could govern a society composed of many religions, cultures and castes. the Mughals?

The text explains that peasants were vital for the economy of the Mughal Empire.

The text explains that how were the debates with religious scholars important in the formation of Akbar’s ideas on governance?

The text explains that why did the Mughals emphasise their Timurid and not their Mongol descent?

The text explains that let’s discuss the stability of the Mughal Empire? from diverse backgrounds and not just Turanis and Iranis?

The text explains that kEYWORDS Mughal primogeniture coparcenary zamindar

The text explains that does this pose a challenge to national integration?

The text explains that do you think that they are as important today?

The text explains that has the gap in the income between the rich and the poor in India changed a great deal from the period of the Mughals?

The text explains that the Mughal Empire left its impact on the different regions of the subcontinent in a variety of ways.

The text explains that find out if it had any impact in the city, village or region in which you live.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II, Chapter 4: The Mughal Empire
